method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Tromelin Island is known in French as Île Tromelin and was formerly called the Isle of Sand.[1] Its present name honours Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette Dauphine, who reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a low-lying, flat coral island of about 1 square kilometre in the Indian Ocean, approximately 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[4] It has been described as an almost “desert island”, with sparse low bushes and no trees.[5] The island’s topography includes wind-shaped coral-sand deposits, and aeolian sedimentation has been identified as an important contributor to its surface form.[3] Strong winds on Tromelin exceeding 8 metres per second are concentrated in a narrow directional sector between 100° and 140°.[3]

The island is uninhabited apart from a meteorological station that has operated since 1949 and is used for cyclone monitoring and regional weather forecasting.[4] It is administered by France as part of the Scattered Islands district of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands.[4] Tromelin also functions as a sea turtle sanctuary, with conservation efforts directed at protecting nesting turtles from invasive species and habitat threats.[4][5]

Tromelin’s ecosystem was severely affected by introduced rats, which reduced native vegetation and left only two surviving seabird species, masked boobies and red-footed boobies.[1][2] After French authorities eradicated the rodents in 2005, native plants began to recover and three bird species returned to the island without additional seabird-attraction measures such as decoys or recorded calls.[1][2] The island is regarded as an important reference ecosystem for environmental research and as an example of island restoration following invasive mammal eradication.[1]

==Fauna and flora==
Tromelin's native vertebrate fauna is dominated by seabirds, while nesting green sea turtles are the main non-avian species; no native terrestrial mammals, reptiles or amphibians are known from the island.[1] Before the impact of invasive Norway rats, the island supported six to eight breeding seabird species, but by 2005 only two booby species remained, both with reduced breeding success.[1] After rat eradication, the breeding seabird community recovered to seven species by 2022, with breeding pairs increasing from 353 to 4,758, a change attributed chiefly to improved local breeding success.[1] The masked booby, represented on Tromelin by the subspecies Sula dactylatra melanops, is among the island's notable breeding seabirds.[1] Between 2017 and 2021, eleven non-breeding seabird species were recorded, including roseate tern, common tern, Saunders's tern, lesser crested tern, brown skua, brown booby, Mascarene petrel, great and lesser frigatebirds, and white-tailed and red-tailed tropicbirds.[3] Great and lesser frigatebirds regularly used the island as a roost, while the two tropicbird species were observed landing or displaying, suggesting possible future colonisation.[3]

The plant community is composed of salt-tolerant littoral species adapted to strong winds, salt spray and limited freshwater.[1] Its native flora historically consisted mainly of the shrub Tournefortia argentea, also known as tree heliotrope, together with fewer than ten herbaceous species.[1] Norway rats reduced native vegetation by consuming seeds, seedlings and foliage, but monitoring after eradication recorded recovery to seven native taxa by 2013, including increased density and height of Tournefortia argentea.[1] A small number of introduced coconut palms persist, but they do not dominate the native vegetation.[1] The recovery of vegetation has also benefited seabirds by improving nesting habitat, while the increase in seabird biomass has enhanced nutrient inputs to adjacent marine ecosystems through guano.[4] Mice remain the only introduced mammals reported on the island, making biosecurity against further invasive species an important conservation concern.[3][1]

==History==
French accounts trace the island’s European discovery to the 1720s, when the navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée recorded it and named it Île de Sable, meaning “Isle of Sand”.[1]  

In July 1761, the French ship Utile was wrecked near the island while carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people.[2] Twenty sailors and at least 70 enslaved passengers died, the latter reportedly trapped below deck because the hatches had been locked or nailed shut.[2] A group of survivors remained on the island for 15 years, and when rescue finally arrived in 1776 only seven women and an eight-month-old child born there were still alive.[2][3] They were rescued by Captain Chevalier de Tromelin, taken to Mauritius, and later freed.[3] The island was subsequently named Tromelin in his honour.[3]  

France dates its claim to sovereignty from 29 November 1776, when the ship Dauphine arrived at the island.[1] Mauritius bases its rival claim on the argument that Tromelin should have passed to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and therefore should not have continued to be administered as a dependency of Réunion.[1] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[1]  

Later accounts by British sailors recorded remains of “stone” houses and carefully arranged graves on the island.[2] Archaeological investigations have uncovered coral-block and compacted-sand dwellings, as well as a large oven containing remains of turtles, birds and shellfish, evidence of the castaways’ long-term efforts to survive.[2] The archaeological work was supported by UNESCO as part of a commemoration of the struggle against slavery.[2] The story has also been examined in reports by archaeologist Max Guérout and in the documentary Tromelin, the Island of Forgotten Slaves, directed by Daniel Vigne.[5]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate under the Köppen climate classification, with warm temperatures throughout the year and limited seasonal variation.[2]  
Its wet season lasts mainly from November to April and is influenced by the intertropical convergence zone, while drier conditions prevail from May to October.[2]  
Average annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, reflecting the island’s low latitude and oceanic setting.[2]  
Annual precipitation is approximately 1,074 mm, with the heaviest rainfall generally occurring during the austral summer, especially around February.[2]  
Prevailing southeast trade winds are a persistent feature of the island’s climate and contribute to high evaporation rates.[2]  
Because of its position in the western Indian Ocean, Tromelin is used for meteorological observation and cyclone monitoring, particularly for systems that may affect Madagascar.[4]
